refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, and system-level optimization. The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation period and scenario scope, making it verifiable and traceable to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption and ton-moved metrics during acceptance testing across representative worksite scenarios (e.g., load types, terrain, operator skill levels). Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target. Require telemetry capture and post-analysis to isolate vehicle efficiency, operational losses, and system-level optimization contributions.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit load moved through efficient drivetrain, hydraulic, and powertrain design.

FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution without energy waste due to control latency or poor situational awareness.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall collect, aggregate, and report energy consumption and productivity metrics (ton_moved) to enable performance tracking against E_target.
FR-EE-004: Safety services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade efficiency below E_target through unnecessary operational constraints or redundant actuation.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Optimize vehicle powertrain efficiency (engine, transmission, hydraulics) and implement real-time energy monitoring with operator feedback loops to guide efficient operation.

DP-EE-B: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that monitors energy consumption, predicts efficiency degradation, and automatically adjusts operational parameters (load distribution, speed, duty cycle) to maintain energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

DP-EE-C: Combine vehicle-level efficiency optimization (DP-EE-A) with system-level telemetry aggregation and operator coaching via teleoperation interface to achieve E_target through design + operational discipline.

DP-EE-D: Establish energy efficiency as a constraint in the safety-critical control loop, ensuring that safety interventions do not cause energy waste, and implement predictive energy budgeting to pre-allocate energy reserves for safety maneuvers.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-C: Combine vehicle-level efficiency optimization (powertrain, hydraulics, drivetrain design) with system-level telemetry aggregation (energy consumption, ton_moved metrics) and operator coaching via teleoperation interface to achieve E_target through integrated design and operational discipline.

potential_sources: Vehicle powertrain efficiency is independent of teleoperation system design; teleoperation provides feedback but does not directly control powertrain parameters.
Telemetry aggregation depends on Vehicle telemetry output (Telemetry interface) but is logically decoupled from vehicle control.
Operator coaching via teleoperation is a soft constraint; it influences behavior but does not create hard coupling to vehicle systems.
Safety services (FR-EE-004) must be evaluated separately to ensure no hidden coupling between safety interventions and energy efficiency.
coupling_check.notes: DP-EE-C exhibits low coupling because it decomposes into independent sub-solutions: (1) vehicle design optimization, (2) telemetry collection, (3) operator interface. Each can be designed and tested independently. Risk: operator behavior is not fully controllable; coaching effectiveness depends on operator discipline and worksite conditions.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle powertrain efficiency gains are achievable within cost and weight constraints; risk: efficiency improvements may require expensive components (e.g., advanced transmissions, hybrid powertrains) that conflict with project budget.

Assumption: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, data aggregation) can be implemented with acceptable latency and reliability; risk: sensor failures or network delays may degrade real-time feedback quality.

Assumption: Operator coaching via teleoperation interface will be adopted and followed by field operators; risk: operators may ignore efficiency guidance under time pressure or fatigue, negating system-level optimization.
Assumption: E_target is achievable under nominal conditions; risk: if E_target is set below the physical limits of the vehicle design, no DP can satisfy the requirement.
Assumption: Safety services do not impose energy penalties; risk: safety-critical actuation (e.g., emergency braking, load stabilization) may consume energy that pushes energy_per_ton above E_target.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-EE-C to explicitly include a trade-off analysis between vehicle efficiency optimization and safety service energy costs. Recommend adding a sub-DP: 'DP-EE-C.1: Allocate energy budget reserves for safety-critical maneuvers and design safety services to minimize energy consumption through efficient actuation and control logic.' This ensures that safety and efficiency are co-optimized rather than treated as competing constraints.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. It is responsible for energy efficiency KPI achievement through coordinated design and operational policies. The module owns the energy_per_ton metric definition, acceptance criteria, and system-level performance reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption (kWh or fuel consumed), ton_moved (load mass × distance), energy_per_ton (calculated metric), vehicle state (speed, load, terrain), operator actions (acceleration, braking, steering). Flows from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to external monitoring and reporting systems (e.g., Site Manager dashboard, acceptance test infrastructure).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Worksite task definitions (load type, destination, terrain, time constraints) from Site Manager or autonomous planning system. Informs vehicle routing and operator guidance to optimize energy efficiency for the assigned task.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts and interventions (e.g., load instability, collision risk, operator override) from Safety Services. Flows into NGHE Solution to trigger energy-aware safety responses; flows out to operator and external safety monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for teleoperation, telemetry aggregation, and safety alert distribution. Supports real-time operator feedback and remote monitoring required for energy efficiency coaching and safety oversight.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level boundary for energy efficiency KPI ownership. It integrates all sub-systems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and owns the energy_per_ton metric definition and acceptance criteria. Minimal diffusion: energy efficiency is a system-level property, not scattered across modules. The only diffusion risk is if energy optimization logic is duplicated in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems rather than coordinated at the NGHE Solution level.

Vehicle module owns powertrain efficiency optimization (DP-EE-C sub-component 1) and telemetry generation. However, energy_per_ton is a system-level metric that depends on operator behavior and teleoperation feedback, which are outside Vehicle scope. If energy efficiency KPI ownership is assigned to Vehicle alone, responsibility for operator coaching and system-level optimization is diffused to Teleoperation and Safety Services, creating unclear accountability.

Teleoperation module owns operator feedback and coaching (DP-EE-C sub-component 3) but does not own vehicle powertrain design or telemetry aggregation. Assigning energy efficiency KPI to Teleoperation alone would diffuse vehicle design responsibility to Vehicle module and metric aggregation to NGHE Solution, creating fragmented ownership.

Safety Services is responsible for ensuring safety does not degrade energy efficiency (FR-EE-004), but does not own the primary energy efficiency optimization mechanisms. Assigning energy efficiency KPI to Safety Services would create high diffusion and unclear accountability for the core efficiency design.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-003 (Energy efficiency KPI) because: (1) it integrates all sub-systems that contribute to energy_per_ton (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (2) it owns the metric definition and acceptance criteria, (3) it can coordinate trade-offs between efficiency and safety, and (4) it provides a single point of accountability for KPI achievement. Sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own specific design parameters (powertrain efficiency, operator feedback, safety-aware actuation) but report energy performance to NGHE Solution for aggregation and verification.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution module may become a 'thin' integration layer if detailed energy optimization logic is not explicitly allocated to sub-modules. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules have clear energy efficiency design requirements (FR-EE-001, FR-EE-002, FR-EE-004) and design parameters.

Risk: Operator behavior is not fully controllable by system design; if operators ignore efficiency coaching, energy_per_ton may exceed E_target despite correct DP and IM design. Mitigation: Establish worksite operational policies (e.g., mandatory efficiency training, incentive structures) as part of acceptance criteria and operational constraints.

Risk: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network) may introduce latency or data loss, degrading real-time feedback quality and operator coaching effectiveness. Mitigation: Specify telemetry reliability and latency requirements in Telemetry interface specification.

Risk: Safety services may impose energy penalties (e.g., emergency braking, load stabilization) that push energy_per_ton above E_target. Mitigation: Allocate energy budget reserves for safety-critical maneuvers and design safety services to minimize energy consumption (DP-EE-C.1).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define energy efficiency sub-requirements for Vehicle (FR-EE-001), Teleoperation (FR-EE-002), and Safety Services (FR-EE-004) modules. Establish a Telemetry interface specification that includes energy consumption, ton_moved, and energy_per_ton metrics with required accuracy, latency, and reliability. Create an energy budget allocation table that reserves energy for safety-critical maneuvers and defines acceptable energy overhead for teleoperation and safety services. This ensures that NGHE Solution ownership is backed by clear sub-module responsibilities and measurable interface contracts.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR-NGHE-003 clearly specifies the energy efficiency outcome (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and evaluation scope (nominal conditions, agreed period, representative scenarios). Selected DP-EE-C (vehicle optimization + telemetry + operator coaching) directly addresses the FR by combining design-level efficiency improvements with operational discipline. The DP is traceable to the FR and does not introduce new functional requirements. Coupling check passes: vehicle, teleoperation, and telemetry are logically independent. Risk: operator behavior is not fully controllable, but this is a known constraint of the problem domain, not a DP deficiency.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Energy optimization logic may be duplicated across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules if interface contracts are not clearly defined. Mitigation: Establish a Telemetry interface that specifies energy consumption and ton_moved metrics; Vehicle module generates these metrics, Teleoperation module consumes them for operator feedback, NGHE Solution aggregates them for KPI reporting.

Risk: Operator coaching effectiveness depends on teleoperation interface quality (latency, feedback clarity); if Teleoperation module does not provide real-time energy feedback, coaching will be ineffective. Mitigation: Specify Telemetry interface latency and feedback format requirements in Teleoperation module design.

Risk: Safety services may impose energy penalties that are not visible to operator or NGHE Solution; if Safety Services module does not report energy costs of safety interventions, system-level energy budgeting is impossible. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface to include safety intervention energy costs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution module contains the energy efficiency KPI ownership and system-level metric aggregation. Vehicle module contains powertrain efficiency design. Teleoperation module contains operator feedback and coaching. Safety Services module contains safety-aware actuation. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not create hidden dependencies. Containment is clean if sub-module responsibilities are clearly specified in their respective FR and DP definitions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy efficiency KPI can be verified through acceptance testing: measure energy consumption (via fuel/power sensors) and ton_moved (via load sensors and GPS) over representative worksite scenarios, calculate energy_per_ton, and compare against E_target. Telemetry interface provides the necessary data for verification. Verification is feasible if sensors are accurate and worksite scenarios are representative of customer use cases.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of energy efficiency responsibility is clear: NGHE Solution owns KPI definition and system-level reporting; Vehicle owns powertrain efficiency; Teleoperation owns operator feedback; Safety Services owns safety-aware actuation. Each module has a clear sub-FR and DP. Allocation is traceable from CR-NGHE-003 to sub-modules via FR-EE-001, FR-EE-002, FR-EE-003, FR-EE-004.

recommended_revisions: Refine Telemetry interface specification to include energy consumption (kWh or fuel consumed), ton_moved (load mass × distance), energy_per_ton (calculated metric), and safety intervention energy costs. Specify required accuracy, latency, and reliability for each metric.

Define energy budget allocation table: allocate energy reserves for safety-critical maneuvers (e.g., emergency braking, load stabilization) and define acceptable energy overhead for teleoperation and safety services. Ensure that E_target is achievable after accounting for safety energy costs.

Establish worksite operational policies and acceptance criteria that include operator training, efficiency incentives, and scenario definitions. Energy efficiency KPI achievement depends on both system design and operational discipline.

Add a sub-DP for Safety Services: 'DP-EE-C.1: Design safety services to minimize energy consumption through efficient actuation, predictive control, and energy-aware intervention logic.' This ensures that safety and efficiency are co-optimized.

decision.justification: Refined FR-NGHE-003 is decomposable into four sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-004) that address vehicle efficiency, teleoperation feedback, system-level telemetry, and safety-aware actuation. Selected DP-EE-C is sound but requires further decomposition to assign design parameters to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. NGHE Solution module is the correct IM boundary for KPI ownership, but sub-module responsibilities must be clarified through the next level of functional decomposition. Proceeding to the next level will establish clear design parameters for each sub-module and define interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that enable system-level energy efficiency optimization.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit load moved through efficient drivetrain, hydraulic, and powertrain design.

FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution without energy waste due to control latency or poor situational awareness.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall collect, aggregate, and report energy consumption and productivity metrics (ton_moved) to enable performance tracking against E_target.
FR-EE-004: Safety services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade efficiency below E_target through unnecessary operational constraints or redundant actuation.

assumptions: Vehicle powertrain efficiency gains are achievable within cost and weight constraints.
Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, data aggregation) can be implemented with acceptable latency and reliability.
Operator coaching via teleoperation interface will be adopted and followed by field operators.
E_target is achievable under nominal conditions and across representative worksite scenarios.
Safety services can be designed to minimize energy consumption without compromising safety.
Worksite operational policies and operator training will support energy efficiency discipline.
Energy efficiency is measured over an agreed evaluation period with representative scenarios defined by customer.
open_risks: Operator behavior is not fully controllable by system design; operators may ignore efficiency coaching under time pressure or fatigue.
Safety-critical actuation (e.g., emergency braking, load stabilization) may consume energy that pushes energy_per_ton above E_target.
Telemetry infrastructure may introduce latency or data loss, degrading real-time feedback quality.
E_target may be set below the physical limits of the vehicle design, making the requirement unachievable.
Sensor accuracy and reliability may be insufficient for precise energy_per_ton calculation.
Worksite scenarios used for acceptance testing may not be representative of customer use cases, leading to performance gaps in the field.
evidence_needed: Baseline energy consumption and ton_moved metrics for current generation equipment (if available) to establish E_target feasibility.
Powertrain efficiency analysis and component selection (engine, transmission, hydraulics) to quantify efficiency gains.
Telemetry sensor specifications (accuracy, latency, reliability) and network infrastructure design to support real-time feedback.
Operator feedback interface design and usability testing to validate coaching effectiveness.
Safety service energy cost analysis to allocate energy budget reserves.
Worksite scenario definitions and acceptance test plan to ensure representative evaluation.
Trade-off analysis between energy efficiency and safety to establish energy budget allocation.
